transcript: Why do tens of tons of extra weight help airlines save millions of dollars?  At first glance, it seems logical to think that the heavier the plane, the higher the costs will be.  But there is a paradox in aviation.  The kerosene stored in the wings is not only used to keep the aircraft in the air.  It also helps to reduce stress on the structure. It sounds absurd, but it's pure physics.  Look at a wing as someone who is seeing the design for the first time.  The fuel is placed precisely in the thinnest part of the aircraft, the part that flexes and vibrates during flight, right before your eyes through the window.  Common sense dictates: "Move the fuel away from the part that shakes."  But the engineers did the exact opposite on purpose, because the fuel inside the wing doesn't make it heavier, it makes it lighter.  See how it works.  During flight, lift pushes the wings upward, while the weight of the fuselage, full of passengers and luggage, pulls them downward.  At the root of the wing, where it connects to the fuselage, an enormous bending moment arises, a force capable of bending the metal in half. Now imagine we fill the wing with kerosene.  The weight of the fuel acts along the entire wing and helps to counteract this flex.  Engineers call this structural relief of the wing. Several tens of tons are added and, paradoxically, a lighter structure is obtained in flight.  Remember the image; the wing is not just a wing.  And another question arises: where exactly is the tank located?  Most people imagine some kind of hidden reservoir inside the wing, but the truth is that it doesn't even exist.  The space between the front and rear spars, the wing's support beams, is already a hermetically sealed support box in itself. This structure is sealed with sealants, and the quosene is stored directly inside.  This system is called a wet wing.  In other words, the plane literally floats on its own fuel.  [music] And this single solution solves three problems at once.  First, it provides that structural relief to the wing.  Secondly, it leaves the entire fuselage free for passengers and cargo.  Third, the fuel is located very close to the engines, allowing for shorter fuel lines.  [music] The price to pay is an extremely complex network of pipes and the risk of fire right next to the engines. In aviation, every advantage comes at a price. To get an idea of ​​how much fuel a wing carries, let's convert the kerosene to a more familiar measure, like refueling at a regular gas station.  Consider a passenger car with a tank that holds about 50 liters. During takeoff and initial climb alone, an Airbus A380 consumes approximately 13 tons of kerosene, the equivalent of about 16,000 liters. In other words, while the plane leaves the runway and climbs to cruising altitude, it consumes enough fuel to fill the tanks of more than 300 cars.  And a full tank on an A380 holds approximately 315,000 liters of fuel.  That's equivalent to more than 6,000 cars filled to the brim.  An entire small town transformed into fuel rocks inside the wings while you simply fasten your seatbelt.  At the gas station, fuel is measured in liters.  1 L more, 1 L less.  What is the difference in aviation?  This lack of accuracy can cost lives.  Quosene expands when heated.  1 L of hot water occupies more space than 1 L of cold water, but contains exactly the same amount of energy and the same mass.  If an airplane were fueled only in liters, the range calculations could be completely wrong.  That's why, in commercial airplanes, fuel is measured in kilograms.  Remember this word: kilograms.  She will reappear later.  Moreover, confusion with units of measurement has caused tragedies more than once.  And we'll talk about one of them later, but first let's ascend to cruising altitude. Outside, the temperature is around -56ºC.  At around -47 degrees Celsius, kerosene begins to solidify.  On long flights over polar regions, it can get so cold that ice crystals begin to form.  What prevents this is a special additive that binds to the water dissolved in the fuel and prevents it from freezing.  And since we're inside the tank, it's worth mentioning that there's something living there.  Water inevitably enters the tanks through condensation.  And at the interface between water and kerosene, a fungus can appear that mechanics have nicknamed the "diesel plague." This organism consumes fuel, produces acids, corrodes aluminum from the inside, and clogs filters with biomass.  The struggle is constant.  Chemical additives and daily tank drainage. Yes, inside the wings of an airplane with hundreds of passengers, there is a small ecosystem of its own.  But kerosene has another function besides serving as fuel.  While waiting its turn to be burned, it also acts as a cooling agent. Through heat exchangers, it helps cool overheated oil in engines and hydraulic systems.  The advantage is obvious, but there is a dark side.  Cold fuel, ice crystals, and a heat exchanger eventually combined years later in one of aviation's most terrifying stories during a landing approach. We'll come back to this later. Imagine that classic situation.  The plane hits turbulence, the wingtip starts to rise and fall, and you grip the armrest, wondering when it will finally come loose.  So you should know that at that very moment, the wing is functioning exactly as designed.  A rigid wing would receive all the force of the gusts directly at the root, precisely where all the tension accumulates and would eventually crack.  The flexible wing acts like a spring.  It absorbs impacts and distributes loads along its entire length.  The composite wings of the Boeing 787 and Airbus A350 are even more flexible than the older aluminum wings.  During ground tests, the wingtip of the 787 can rise up to about 7.5 m.  And to prove that all of this works, the engineers do something that nobody would want to see during a flight.  They break their wing.  And no, this is not vandalism, it's a mandatory step in the certification process.  First, the structure is subjected to 100% of the maximum load it will likely face during the entire lifespan of the aircraft. Then, the effort is increased to 150%. If the ASA can withstand all of this without failing, it will be approved.  In the final test of the Boeing 777, the wing withstood 154% of the predicted load, and only then did the section closest to the tip break.  The bang was so loud that watching the video makes you want to duck.  For the engineers, however, it wasn't a failure, it was a triumph.  The wing broke exactly where it should have, and only after exceeding the limit for which it had been designed.   In the early 1950s, the British aircraft The Ravel Comet was the first commercial jet in history.  It was an elegant machine, far ahead of its time, and had almost rectangular windows.  The problem was that in the corners of these windows, the structural stress concentrated with each pressurization and depressurization cycle.  The metal was becoming fatigued, small, invisible cracks were slowly growing, and one day the planes began to disintegrate in mid-flight.  After a series of accidents and a lengthy investigation, windows began to be manufactured in an oval shape.  Since then, all commercial airplanes in the world have used windows with rounded corners, but there's one detail that practically every passenger has seen and almost no one has understood.  Look carefully out the airplane window.  There is a small hole at the bottom of the glass .  The window is actually made up of three layers.  The outer layer withstands the entire pressure difference between the cabin and the air outside.  The intermediate layer acts as a backup, in case the outer layer has a problem. And that small hole serves to relieve pressure on that intermediate layer, reducing the stress it can withstand.  In addition, it helps eliminate moisture to prevent the window from fogging up.  Now you know what it's for.  And since we 're talking about weight, there's another interesting detail.  A large commercial aircraft can take off weighing more than 50 tons above the maximum permitted landing weight.  If he tried to land like that, he could seriously damage the landing gear.  But what if an emergency arises right after takeoff?  There are two options, and both waste fuel. Aircraft equipped with jettison systems release tens of tons of kerosene through nozzles installed on the wings, spreading it into the air until a safe landing weight is reached.  Those that don't have this system have to remain flying in circles near the airport, burning excess fuel in their engines.  Sometimes this takes hours. When the fuel ran out, disasters and lessons followed.  Do you remember the chirramas I asked you to memorize?  Now it's time to find out why.  On July 23, 1983, Air Canada Flight 143 transformed into a glider at an altitude of approximately 12 km .  During refueling, there was some confusion between kilograms and pounds.  The plane received only half the fuel it needed.  Over the province of Ontario, both engines stopped.  Commander Robert Pearon had an unexpected advantage.  Before becoming an airline pilot, he was a glider pilot.  Without any thrust from the engines, he steered the enormous plane in gliding flight.  The crew remembered an old airfield in Guinley that had been converted into a racetrack . That day there were competitions taking place and hundreds of spectators at the venue.  Even without engines, flaps, and virtually no hydraulic system, the plane managed to land and did not hit anyone on the ground.  All 69 people on board survived.  Do you also remember the combination of cold fuel, ice, and heat exchangers?  On January 17, 2008, a Boeing 777 was approaching Hrow Airport after an 11- hour flight from Beijing.  When the aircraft was at an altitude of approximately 200 meters, both engines stopped responding almost simultaneously, and the fuel tanks were full.  The plane crashed onto a grassy area before the runway and its landing gear was destroyed.  Miraculously, no one died.  The investigation revealed that during the long flight, the fuel had cooled down too much.  Ice crystals accumulated and clogged the heat exchangers.  After that incident, the entire 777 fleet, equipped with Rolls-Royce engines, received modifications to that system.  Sometimes, however, what kills is not the ice or the numbers, but a single unspoken word.  On January 25, 1990, an Avianca Boeing 70 was awaiting clearance to land in New York. Due to the delays, the aircraft remained in the air much longer than planned.  The fuel was running out.  The crew repeatedly informed air traffic controllers that the situation was critical, but at no point did they utter the keyword "emergency."  In U.S. aeronautical terminology, only this word guarantees absolute priority.  The air traffic controllers did not understand the seriousness of the situation.  The plane crashed approximately 25 km from the runway.  The fuel simply ran out.  Of the 158 people on board, 73 died.  After that, international procedures for fuel emergencies became much more stringent and standardized.  A few years earlier, another accident had taught a different lesson.  On December 28, 1978, a United Airlines DC8 was circling Portland due to a problem with its landing gear.  The crew became so focused on the mechanical failure that they ignored repeated warnings about the fuel.  When the four engines started failing one after the other, it was already too late.  The plane crashed in a residential area just 10 km from the airport.  10 people died.  The investigation revealed that the co-pilot and the flight engineer had noticed the problem, but did not feel comfortable confronting the captain.  This accident gave rise to the concept of CRM, teamwork and communication training, which is now part of pilot training worldwide.  And now we come to the case where the fuel stored at ASA played a central role, exactly what we've been talking about from the beginning.  On July 25, 2000, an Air France Concorde took off from Paris.  During the takeoff run, one of the tires ran over a metal strip that had fallen from another plane.  The tire exploded, and a piece of rubber weighing several kilograms struck the underside of the wing with enormous force.  Inside was the fuel tank.  The impact did not directly puncture the tank, but it generated a powerful pressure wave in the fuel that eventually ruptured the structure from the inside.  Burning kerosene began to gush from the wing.  The plane took off already surrounded by fire.  90 seconds later it fell. 113 people died.  In this case, the great advantage of the wet wing turned into its downfall.  A single rupture was enough to trigger the tragedy. Now let's talk about aircraft with truly extraordinary fuel systems .  The SR71 Blackbird, the fastest jet airliner in history, flying at over 3 Mach 3. But parked on the ground, it looked like a candidate for the scrapyard.  The fuel was literally running down the fuselage and forming puddles under the aircraft.  And that wasn't a defect, it was part of the design.  The SR71's titanium structure was designed to withstand temperatures close to 300ºC during flight.  When heated, the metal expanded and the gaps between the panels closed, making the aircraft airtight.  On the ground, which was still cold, these gaps remained open.  Therefore, [music] the Blackbird would take off almost empty, refuel in flight immediately, and only depart for the mission after warming up. And the fuel was special too. Quosene JP7 was so stable that a simple flame could not easily ignite it .  In addition, it was used to help cool the overheated fuselage .  To make it burn inside the engines, it was necessary to inject a special substance that ignited instantly upon contact with air.  In contrast, there was a legend of its own. The MiG-25 interceptor, capable of flying close to Mac 3, reached altitudes of almost 37 km in record-breaking flights.  Its engines normally consumed kerosene, but the radar was cooled by a mixture of alcohol and water.  The aircraft was carrying approximately 250 liters of technical alcohol. Because of this, it earned the nickname " flying grocery store."  According to reports from mechanics, some of this stock mysteriously disappeared while still on the ground. And we return to the Concord, which perhaps possessed the most ingenious trick of all. At supersonic speeds, the aircraft's center of lift shifted approximately 2 meters backward.  Without compensation, the nose would tend to rise excessively. To solve this, the Concorde pumped several tons of fuel from the front tanks to the rear ones. Thus, the center of gravity followed the displacement of the lift.  In this case, the fuel ceased to be just fuel; it transformed into a huge, movable counterweight, adjusting the aircraft's balance in mid-flight.   Do you think a tanker truck carries all the fuel needed to power an airplane?  In major airports, this is already a thing of the past.  Beneath the apron, where the aircraft are parked, lies a vast network of underground pipes, known as the fire hydrant system.  The refueling vehicle simply approaches the aircraft, connects to a cap on the ground, and opens a valve. From there, the fuel flows under pressure directly to the wings.  And these pipelines don't start at the airport.  Many of them are linked to refineries located hundreds of kilometers away.  An airport the size of Hitro consumes approximately 20 million liters of kerosene per day. It's literally an underground river of fuel flowing non-stop.  But now comes the most interesting part.  For nearly a century, aviation has perfected an idea: storing fuel directly inside the wings, which are already structures capable of supporting heavy loads.  And hydrogen threatens to completely change that logic. Liquid hydrogen needs to be kept at approximately -253ºC, very close to absolute zero.  For this, thickly insulated cylindrical tanks , similar to enormous thermos bottles, are needed.  And there's simply no way to fit structures of that type inside a thin, flexible wing.  Therefore, tanks will have to switch to firing squads.  And then the wing, which we've spent this entire video analyzing as a living organism carrying fuel, balancing forces, cooling systems, and actively participating in the aircraft's operation, will revert to being just a wing.  This change will require new aerodynamics, new centers of gravity, and new engineering challenges. If you're flying these days, you might be living through the final years of the wet wing era, that brilliant idea that helped make modern aviation possible and that, for almost a century, was silently present in virtually every flight you've ever taken.
